name: Discount allowed and received
intent: Ledgers that record trade or cash discounts given to customers or received from vendors. These are contra-revenue or contra-expense adjustments arising from commercial settlements, not payments for any service or work. No TDS applies because no service is being rendered — it's simply a price reduction.

name: Round off Ledger
intent: Ledgers used to absorb fractional differences (paise-level) that arise due to GST rounding, invoice rounding, or accounting adjustments. These are purely mathematical balancing entries with no underlying transaction or payee. No TDS applies as there is no payment being made to any party.

name: Bad Debts & its provision
intent: Ledgers that record irrecoverable receivables being written off or a provision being created for doubtful debtors. This is an internal accounting recognition of loss — no actual payment flows out to any vendor or party. No TDS applies as there is no payment being made to any person.
keywords: provision for bad and doubtful debts

name: Profit Loss on sale of asset
intent: Ledgers that capture the gain or loss arising when a fixed asset is sold — the difference between sale proceeds and book value. This is a notional accounting entry, not a payment to a vendor or contractor. No TDS applies as no amount is being paid to any deductee.
keywords: profit on sale of fixed assets
loss on sale of fixed assets
profit on disposal of fixed assets
loss on disposal of fixed assets
profit on sale of plant and machinery
loss on sale of plant and machinery
capital gain on sale of assets
capital loss on sale of assets

name: Prior period expense
intent: Ledgers used to record expenses that belong to an earlier accounting period but are being accounted for now. The TDS liability, if any, would have been applicable at the time the original expense was incurred and paid.

name: Write off Ledgers
intent: Ledgers used to write off assets, stock, advances, or balances that are no longer recoverable or relevant. Like bad debts, this is an internal adjustment entry — no payment is going out to any external party. No TDS applies as there is no payment being made.
keywords: capital work in progress written off

name: Bank Charges
intent: Ledgers that record fees levied directly by banks — such as service charges, cheque processing fees, NEFT/RTGS charges, or account maintenance fees. Banks are not deductees under TDS for routine service charges they themselves levy.

name: Late Fees & Penalties
intent: Ledgers recording statutory penalties, late filing fees, interest on delayed tax payments, or penal charges levied by government authorities (GST, Income Tax, ROC, etc.). Payments to government and statutory bodies are outside the TDS framework. Additionally, penalties are not deductible business expenses and carry no TDS obligation.

name: GST Ledgers in expenses
intent: Ledgers representing GST components — CGST, SGST, IGST, UTGST — that appear on the expense side due to ITC reversal, blocked credit, or GST paid under reverse charge. These are tax liability entries payable to the government, not payments to vendors for services. No TDS applies on tax components paid to the government. If a ledger has name 'purchase-GST' then this is NOT a GST blocklist ledger — it is a purchase ledger that happens to have GST in the name. Look at the real intent of the expense; only ledgers solely representing a GST tax component are excluded.

name: Income Tax
intent: Ledgers recording advance tax, self-assessment tax, or income tax provisions. These are direct tax payments made by the company to the government on its own income. No TDS is deductible on tax payments made to the government. Include only ledgers that solely relate to Income tax. Important: a ledger called 'Tax Audit Fees' or 'Tax Consultancy Fees' is NOT this category — those are professional fees paid to a CA/consultant and TDS u/s 194J applies; such ledgers must NOT be blocked.

name: ESI & PF Ledger
intent: Ledgers recording employer contributions to Employee State Insurance and Provident Fund. These are statutory payroll-linked contributions paid to government-managed funds (ESIC and EPFO). Contributions to recognised provident funds and ESI are specifically outside the TDS framework and are governed by separate statutes entirely.
